While we wait for the actual inflation numbers to come out, markets don't have that luxury, and the early and perhaps best indicator of market expectations on inflation is showing up in interest rates. The graph below looks at 3-month and 10-year US treasuries over the course of March 2026:

The 3-month treasury bill rate has barely budged over the month, moving from 3.67% on February 27, 2026, to 3.70% on March 31, 2026, but the ten-year bond rate saw a much bigger increase from 3.97% on February 27, 2026, to 4.30% on March 31, 2026. The biggest increases in rates are in the intermediate maturities, with the 2-year and 5-year rates rising by 0.41% over the course of the month. If you view interest rates, as I do, as driven by expected inflation and expected real growth, the most plausible reading is that the market sees an increase in inflation that is persistent. If you are a Fed-watcher, though, your reading may be that the rise in oil prices has tied the hands of the Fed, lowering the likelihood that the Fed Funds rate will be cut in the coming months, but that would leave you with a puzzle to resolve. Since the Fed Funds rate, an overnight bank borrowing rate, has its biggest impact on the short end of the maturity spectrum, how do you explain the fact that short-term rates have not changed much?

The increase in interest rates is not just specific to the US, with rises in rates across other currencies, as you can see in this graph of ten-year euro, yen, and yuan rates:

The Japanese yen and euro rates are up significantly over the month, but the yuan rate has seen no change in March 2026. Staying with the market narrative, this indicates higher inflation across countries and currencies.

While there are many who are speculating on what higher inflation and oil prices will do to the economy, and investment banks and data services (See Moody's, Goldman Sachs) have been rushing to update their forecasts for the US economy, the market has not been in as much of a rush to make the judgment. The economy was showing signs of fatigue coming into March 2026, with anemic growth and employment numbers, and it is possible that the oil price shock will tip it over into a recession.

The Price of Risk

The heightened uncertainty generated by war and its consequences has played its way out not just in oil prices and treasury rates, but in the prices that investors charge for risk. In a month when the clash between greed and risk took front stage, with the balance shifting often on a minute-by-minute basis during the trading day, we also see increases in the price that investors charge for taking risk in both equity and bond markets. In the equity market, that price of risk is the equity risk premium, a topic that I talked about extensively in this post and paper, with the argument that a good measure of this risk premium will be forward-looking and dynamic. My implied equity risk premium estimates tried to capture the changes in equity risk premiums on a daily basis, and completing the assessments for the entire month, here is what the equity risk premiums looked like in March 2026:

The surprise here is not that the equity risk premium rose over the course of the month, expected given what was happening in the Middle East, but that it rose so modestly. In fact, over the course of March, the implied equity risk premium for the S&P 500 rose from 4.37% on February 27, 2026, to 4.77% at the close of trading on March 31, 2026, an increase of 0.40% for the month.

In the bond market, the price of risk is the bond default spread, and in the graph below, I look at default spreads for seven bond rating classes from AAA to C (and below):

Here again, the spreads increased over the month, but only modestly, even at the lowest ratings classes. Thus, the BBB default spread over the 10-year treasury rose only 0.08% during the month, from 1.07% on February 27, 2026, to 1.15% on March 31, 2026, and the high-yield spread (for CCC and below) increased from 9.50% at the start of March 2026 to 10.10% at the end of the month.

The third proxy for risk is the volatility index (the VIX) for US equities, and that measure rose during the course of March 2026:

During March 2026, the VIX rose from 19.86 at the start of the month to 25.25 by the end of the month, an increase much smaller than the increases we saw in March 2020 (COVID) or in the first week of April 2025 (tariff week).

With the caveat that this is still mid-narrative, the bottom line from the movement in all of these risk measures is that while the market had a bad month, much of the marking down in equity values can be attributed to real concerns about higher inflation and economic damage and is not the result of panic selling, at least in the aggregate. To back this up, I took a look at two collectibles - gold, which has a history of holding its value or even increasing during crises and panics in financial markets, and bitcoin, which has not had that history so far but is marketed by its advocates as a potential hedge:

Gold was down 10.42% during March 2026, uncommon for a crisis month, but bitcoin was up 3.30% during the month, and it is entirely in keeping with bitcoin investors marching to their own music, though it will be interesting to see how this dynamic plays out, as this repricing continues.

Effect across Geographies

The war is in the Middle East, but there is no place to hide from its effects. To see how the war has played out in different regions, I looked at the change in aggregate market cap, in US dollar terms, in March 2026:

You may be surprised to see Africa & the Middle East and Eastern Europe & Russia show up as the best-performing markets, with about 2% decreases in market capitalization, but it reflects the dual impact of the war. While it has wreaked havoc across the Middle East, the higher oil prices that it has brought with it are providing upside for oil producers that offsets some of the damage.

Since these dollar returns reflect local market performance as well as the strength/weaknesses of their currencies against the dollar, I looked at the US dollar's performance in March 2026:

I know that I am piling on at this stage, but I do compute equity risk premiums for other countries twice a year, once at the start and once mid-year. Given how much March has shaken up the status quo, I will make an exception and re-estimate equity risk premiums by country, updating both my mature market premium (which I estimate from the S&P 500) as well as the country ratings, default spreads, and country equity risk premiums for other countries.

It is worth noting that these equity risk premiums are computed based upon sovereign ratings, which are slow to change as the world convulses. That has been an issue with my ERP computations for Russia and Ukraine since 2022, with the rating for the former withdrawn and the rating for the latter frozen at Ca (Moody's); I have used a country risk score from PRS for the last two years to update Russia's equity risk premium and have done the same for Ukraine in this update. You can see the same issues now, with the war in Iran rocking the boat, and at least for the Middle East, there is reason to believe that the ratings may understate country risk. While none of the countries in the war zone have seen their sovereign rating change (yet), these countries have market estimates of sovereign default risk in the form of sovereign CDS spreads, I looked at the movement in those spreads during the course of the month:

Not surprisingly, market measures of default risk are more sensitive to war effects, and have risen for much of the Middle East as worries have mounted, with bigger increases in Qatar, the UAE, and Turkey than in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait. The United States has also seen a surge in its sovereign CDS spread, and the global sovereign CDS spreads have risen about 12% in the first quarter of 2026. Using these sovereign CDS spreads as measures of default spreads for this part of the world may yield more realistic equity risk premiums.

What now?

I noted at the start of this post that the uncertainties that manifested during March 2026 about the direction, duration, and effects of war are still unresolved and perhaps have even grown as we start April. As investors try to navigate their way through this period, here are the questions that you will need to answer to decide where you fall in the continuum between complacency and full-blown panic:

In the complacency scenario, the war ends quickly (in days or weeks, rather than months), the damaged infrastructure is repaired quickly, and the new regime in Iran is viewed favorably by the rest of the world, allowing the sanctions on the country to be removed. It is likely that oil prices will drop, perhaps even to below pre-war levels, as Russian and Iranian oil is freely bought and sold. In the full-scale panic scenario, the war continues for months, with lasting damage to infrastructure and supply chains, and Iran's new government stays sanctioned. Oil prices are likely to stay high and perhaps even go higher, the global economy will be kneecapped, and parts of the Middle East (Dubai and Abu Dhabi) that had created a business- and tourist-friendly setting will struggle to find their balance.

In either case, the war has shaken up the status quo, and I see lasting consequences that go well beyond oil. The capital flows from the oil-rich countries, which have flowed generously to everything from AI start-ups to Premier League clubs, will shrink, creating down-market effects. That money, and the funds that were set aside to build vanity projects, from ski resorts in the deserts to state-of-the-art cities, will be redirected to building pipelines and securing the flow of oil. Global politics has also been roiled, and even if the war ends quickly, there is damage that has been done to partnerships and security agreements that cannot be undone.

Datasets

  1. Equity risk premium for S&P 500, by day (March 2026)
  2. Equity risk premiums, by country (updated April 1, 2026)

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Bath & Body Works: Still Staying On The Sidelines
data.description:
Summary

I upgraded to a hold rating for Bath & Body Works, Inc. (BBWI) in December last year, with my key thesis being that valuation has already gone down as I expected. It seems clear that management is now moving the Consumer First Formula into execution, which at least gives investors tangible progress to track. That said, the core demand trend is still soft. So, for me, this is still a neutral story.

Turnaround efforts are proceeding

A brief recap on my view on BBWI previously: I believed demand was weak, inventory was too high, margins looked to have downside, and the turnaround plan was still pretty much theoretical. None of those have completely gone away, but what has changed is that BBWI is finally doing things that investors can track tangibly.

Perhaps the most important one is that BBWI has started making real changes across product, distribution, and the customer experience. For one, they have cut SKUs by 10%, launched on Amazon on February 20, and lowered the free shipping threshold from $100 to $50 (all part of the Consumer First Formula). To be fair, these are not major game changers, but they do show management's willingness to fix things and make it easier for customers to shop. Relative to offering straight-up discounts/promotions, I'd say this is a more sustainable approach as it targets the shopping experience itself rather than just pulling demand forward. In other words, I see that BBWI is making an effort to remove purchase friction, simplify the offer, and widen access to the brand. If this works, it should help support demand in a way that is less dependent on constantly training customers to wait for promotions.

I also thought that the category detail helps in painting a more positive picture of the business. The good news here is that weakness was not broad-based across the whole business. In the recent call, they explicitly said body care declined mid-single-digits and also admitted that seasonal collections did not resonate for the first time in several years. They also said home fragrance grew low single digits, candles did well, soaps and sanitizers also grew low single digits, and Champagne Toast had its strongest year ever. This disclosure is huge because it indicates that BBWI does have strong demand in categories in which it did well. It looks more like the biggest issue sits in body care, which is an easier problem to fix vs. trying to rejuvenate growth across the board (which would imply overall consumer demand weakness).

The base business is still weak, which is why I stay neutral

That said, the reality is the core business is still not in good shape. If we strip out the benefit of BBWI's broader promotional activity, the business has been trending down ~3% (this is per management). And importantly, management noted that the -3% trend should serve as a baseline for 2026. This guide effectively tells us that, as much as there are ways to fix things and some plans are moving to the execution phase, the underlying demand problem (which could be for 1 category or multiple) is still not fixed. Viewed another way, what I mean is that a business can look better run and still not be truly better. Unfortunately, it also seems like things are going to get worse before we see any improvement, as management guided Q1 net sales to be -6% to -4% and framed 2026 as a year of investment behind the Consumer First Formula.

On the latter point, while I did say the efforts made are better relative to giving outright promotions, I don't mean to say BBWI is not using promotions. Unfortunately, here, management said the 2026 plan assumes promotional levels comparable to 2025's. I guess that is better than saying discounting needs to get even more aggressive, but it certainly doesn't suggest that the underlying demand is getting better (i.e., the initiatives implemented have probably not shown major signs of success yet). If it is, BBWI would be pulling back on promotions.

All in all, I believe BBWI is still in the stage where it is spending to fix the brand while the P&L remains under pressure. I don't think the market is missing out on this. If anything, I'd say this is exactly why the stock remains in the “show-me” phase. I would imagine investors to be willing to look through a messy year, but only if they see clear signs that the underlying trend is starting to turn. So far, we are not there yet.

Valuation

From a valuation perspective, I think the picture is largely the same as last December. NTM PE has bounced around the ~7x level (this is the -1 standard deviation), which I believe is because the market is pricing in both the positive and negative updates. In this case, it was great to see real proof that the Consumer First Formula is going into the execution phase and also that the demand weakness is not broad-based (so there is a way to fix it). However, it is also a fact that we have not seen any real demand recovery, and it is very likely that 2026 will be a reset year (i.e., unlikely to have a V-shaped recovery). While there is a path to BBWI eventually showing solid recovery, I expect the market to continue focusing on the near-term recovery trends and BBWI to trade at the same level as peers with similar near-term growth outlooks. For example, Coty Inc. (COTY) trades at 6x NTM PE with 1% revenue growth over the NTM, which is similar to BBWI.

Conclusion

To conclude, I think BBWI is in a better operational place than it was previously. SKU cuts, the Amazon launch, and a lower shipping threshold all point to management finally acting. The problem is that the business itself is still not showing real recovery. Core demand remains weak, and 2026 looks more like a reset year. For these reasons, I am staying neutral.

data.title: Nokia: Why This Upswing Is Dangerous
data.description:
Whenever a company moves contrary to what I would consider its inclination from fundamentals and earnings, I pay attention. Early on, when Nokia (NOK) started to rise, I didn't pay it that much attention. And a year later, when it had risen higher, I had other companies to look at, and I figured the valuation would normalize when it became clear that the company had little to support its upside. But this has not happened - instead, Nokia has continued to rise despite there, in my view, being very little to support such a lateral movement.

We've now reached a point close to $9/share for the NOK ticker here in the NYSE, where I believe things are getting very dangerous. SA analysts have the company at "STRONG BUY", Wall Street is at "BUY", and Quant is at "HOLD". It's not the most diverse set of recommendations I have seen, but given the company's poor ratings for both growth and valuation, one can argue the question as to what exactly supports this upswing. My argument would be that there might be a risk that there is little to support it.

My purpose in this article will be to review what is supporting the company's relatively recent upswing and in what context it could be sustainable (or keep going up from here on). It's always important, unless you're completely on the side of looking only at future growth possibilities, no matter how little they are grounded in reality or numbers, to understand clearly where you believe that the upside in your investments would be coming from. Unless your assumption is that the market is going to premiumize a stock because of its prospects and potential (in which case I do not believe I have that much to offer you here - I look at reported numbers that the company has actually earned), this should be of significant interest to you.

Because if you're already invested in Nokia, it might be a question that you're also asking yourself (I know I would) of whether it's time to sell.

Nokia - What has driven the upswing since 2025

Nokia was on a strong performance kick until about August 2025 last year, when it promptly turned around, fell, and crashed to less than $4.1/share. Then it started to rise - and hasn't really stopped rising since. It now presents with a 100%+ RoR since August 2025. Those who invested in the company should be congratulated. However, those who invested also need to be aware that this investment and upswing were done at a time when Nokia as a company experienced negative earnings growth. In fiscal 2025, which is the last period we have available to us, the company saw an AEPS drop of over 15%.

As things stand, with the current forecasts of 2026 and an AEPS drop of only 5.6%, I wouldn't exactly bet on the company to perform in any way impressively. Based on historical forecast accuracy, Nokia is not a company that often impresses with its numbers. More than 40% of the time, the company negatively misses its estimates.

So, first off, why did the company's 2025 results drop so far?

It depends on how you measure it. I work mostly with GAAP/IFRS numbers. Diluted EPS was down about 26%, and reported EPS was down almost 50% for the fiscal year. A few, not entirely unexpected reasons for this drop.

First off, the company spent billions of euros to buy Infinera (we'll go back to this one later) because the company now wants to compete in AI/optical networking. This move obviously resulted in very high costs and balance sheet adjustments. This was not made easier by very difficult comps - in 2024, the company had a surge from India due to rollouts. This, in turn, in 2025 completely disappeared, which resulted in a massive gap in revenue the company found itself unable to fill.

Also, like any company today, Nokia is currently in the midst of a restructuring. This move has left the company cutting 14,000 jobs, saving billions, but those costs for removing those employees—all of those—were put on the results for 2025.

We find a few signs that things weren't actually as bad as that - FCF moved up to €1.5B, which was very solid. Provided that you view 2025 as a bit of a fiscal reset, which many bullish analysts seem wont to do, and provided you don't view some of the company's future, or fundamentals, as challenging (which, again, many of them do not seem to do), then I can see some of the arguments, at least, for a positive thesis on Nokia.

I just don't agree with it.

Let me go through the reasons I think it is a bad idea.

First, the most obvious ones. Nokia isn't fundamentally the best company, as things stand. Its credit rating is only BBB-. The company has experienced significant reductions in net cash due to restructuring, and if the company wants to pursue this AI push, then these costs are only starting. There are also costs for the China integration, which are already expected to create significant costs.

Part of the problem is that on paper, the company's numbers look good. Debt/equity is actually below 0.22, but if you look at other metrics, these are quicker to suggest some underlying issues. Significant reduction in operating margin to less than 9% (from over 14%), and some of the financial indicators, such as the Altman Z-score, put the company in areas where they are considered less stable.

Analysts tend to forget that Nokia must spend upwards of €4B+ per year on R&D simply to remain competitive. Not spending that risks allowing competition - Samsung, Huawei, or others - to get ahead. Because of this, Nokia does not have as much money on hand, or as impressive an amount of cash flow, as you may believe that it does. The company's earnings, almost flat for going on over 15 years, confirm this.

The reason that I am hesitant about Nokia is that I know (from research and analysis) that it will take something truly significant for the company to get out of this cycle and show solid, growing, year-over-year earnings. It needs to become a market leader, much like it was in 2006. Why do you think earnings and share price were so good back then?

Nokia wasn't just a phone business at the time - it was the center of the world of communications technology. In 2007, in a single year, the company sold products for over €50B and had a global monopoly of almost 40%. At the time, it was the Apple of the segment.

I consider this monumentally unlikely to be repeated.

And that, dear reader, is why I am so hesitant about this recent surge. Bulls would have you believe (some of them) that there is massive, untapped potential in Infinera that Nokia bought for about €2B about a year ago.

Why?

Infinera was bought at a massive (over 20%) premium to its realistic value. It was bought when Infinera did not make money - it made a GAAP loss of $150M in 2024.

I believe Nokia in fact, fell into the trap that many companies did back then - buying "growth" at any price, in this case, too high, and taking on not only a problematic business but one that requires significant turnaround efforts. A good M&A is one that as seamlessly as possible inserts into your operations, becoming accretive within the year, and boosting your earnings very quickly. That is not the case here.

As someone who has followed Nokia for years, it also reflects, to me, a very ironic move for the company. Like Ericsson (ERIC), the company has been in a "frustrated" position for some time. It has sold off optical and cable assets in order to focus on its mobile and cell tower operations, which I was against when it happened.

So what is the company doing by buying Infinera? It's buying back a sort of segment or operation that it once sold or could have had. Further, both Nokia and Infinera, in fact, have competing optical products, which makes me wonder what exactly the company is doing here. I believe by confusing customers, Nokia is risking loss of revenues to competition - this is not a segment where Nokia has exclusivity in any way - Cisco (CSCO) is certainly in here.

My view on Infinera as a risk is based not on opinion/subjectivity alone, but on sound, objective financial facts. The reason Nokia's operating margin went down was due to Infinera losses. The premium paid versus the operating losses on the part of its target means the company bought an unprofitable asset. I view the objective proof of a successful M&A as being margin expansion, not just revenue add - you can always add revenue inorganically. By failing to materialize a positive margin, I argue that Nokia has already failed, for the time being, the first test.

Furthermore, if Nokia's addition of Infinera was indeed a game changer for the company, then why is the company not outperforming peers like Ciena? Research and analytics show us that Nokia is considered behind Ciena, even with the M&A.

How does this play with valuation?

Valuation for Nokia - not great here, given the premiumization

I view this entire period of 8 months as a bit of hype-based upside. Aside from the growth from add-ons to revenues, most of the company's upside is based not on sound financial numbers but instead on speculation and estimates on "how much AI is going to grow".

I consider such exuberance at best a poor substitute for actual results. All the partnership with Nvidia is, I argue, based on speculation. The company's cash reserves are, as mentioned above, subject to a significant annual R&D budget, which, based on the company's AI ambitions, restructuring, and other macro challenges, will be strained further. Unless the company manages to eke out profit from its AI assumptions, it will be lumped with other AI-centric companies that, as of yet, are failing to make a profit. As you might be able to read "between the lines", I don't view the shift to AI markets as a necessary positive. Nokia has an incredibly cyclical market to serve, and a slowdown in NA, which I believe is likely, would impact Nokia significantly.

The positive expectations entirely disregard even the potential for competition like ERIC and Huawei, especially in mobile networks.

So, let me put my assumptions in light of actual company numbers. I've tried to work out exactly how much of the company's profit could be tied to AI and, from there, how much it would take to reach the company's own goals and how realistic those, in turn, would be.

Based on 2025 results, the company's Network Infrastructure segment includes Infinera, which is where the AI capital sits. This segment, looking at AI & Cloud customer revenue, was about 15-16% of the total infrastructure sales. Sales do not mean profit, but by year-end 2025, Nokia highlighted about €2.4B in orders related to AI and Cloud (about €1.2B from network infrastructure).

Since Nokia fails to disclose segment- or AI-specific net profit, I have to work with EBIT margin - at a segment operating margin of about 10% from a €1.22B revenue in Network infrastructure, we come to about €120M in operating profit from AI in a year. That makes AI profit, in any scenario, a very small segment compared to their patent licensing and 5G radio networks. While AI is offsetting some telecom revenue declines, it is in no way, by profit or revenues, a sector that can compete with legacy. Nor do I believe Infinera has the potential to change this.

I therefore view the upswing in valuation as hollow, ungrounded, driven by speculation, and dangerous. It represents a 25x P/E (normalized) at $8.82/share for a company that typically trades at 12-15x P/E. At 15x P/E, the highest value for 2026E, given the 5% AEPS improvement, would be around $5.4/share. Even if you assume double-digit growth, which I consider unlikely given also the company's own estimate for R&D, you'd still have a hard time arguing for any PT higher than $5/share if you want to get a 15% annualized RoR. This is made more difficult by the company's relatively low yield - only 1.34%.

Conclusively, I don't view the company's recent surge based on actual results and fundamentals and realistic estimates to be based on anything aside from exuberance. I don't invest based on exuberance. I view the highest possible share price target for the company, provided that 15% annualized is a hurdle you view as attractive, as about $5/share. At that share price, I am accepting the company's growth estimates.

Anything higher than $5/share, therefore, would need to either be justified by a higher historical P/E than 15x, which I consider to be unlikely, or an even higher AEPS growth than 11% on average - again, unrealistic in my view.

Based on this, I give the company a "HOLD" rating and say that it is a rotation target.

Thesis

Nokia is an attractive communications equipment company that is moving into cloud and AI with its recent integration. I believe this may result in increased earnings, but I believe that to be 100% priced in and more above $5/share. Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a $5/share price target to clear my hurdle of 15% annualized, which the company is not doing.

I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a "HOLD" rating and wait until we see a better valuation.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a price target of $5/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2026.

data.title: Goldman Sachs BDC: Downside Risks Remain If NAV Growth Doesn't Improve
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the Goldman Sachs BDC (GSBD), I issued a hold rating due to the vulnerabilities related to a high interest rate environment. At the time, GSBD traded at an attractive discount to NAV valuation, but I still didn't think it was a good time to accumulate. Since then, the share price has declined by about 8.5% as conditions in the debt markets worsened. It's been a few quarters since I assessed GSBD, so I wanted to review its current valuation, performance, and outlook for the remainder of the year. While GSBD has held up better than some peer BDCs, I believe that there are still some downside risks for investors to consider.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that GSBD's share price has declined by more than 22%. Most of this decline happened following the downturn of the debt markets in Q4 of last year. Even when including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return sits at a loss of about 7.6% over the same time frame. Due to the pullback, GSBD now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 15.8%. The latest earnings report indicates that the distributions are supported by earnings, but this comes after a prior cut in payouts.

GBDC has now fallen to its deepest discount to NAV valuation ever! Despite this, I believe that it can still get a bit worse before things eventually recover. Interest rates remain elevated, and with the escalation of global conflict, rising unemployment, and a market selloff related to technology, there's a chance that future rate cuts may be delayed. Therefore, I anticipate that GSBD's growth potential is suppressed since a high cost of debt limits the opportunities available to management.

Q4 Earnings

According to the latest portfolio overview, GSBD has investments at a fair value of $3.26B. Unlike traditional equity funds, GSBD invests in a diverse range of debt securities, so its success is reliant on a borrower's ability to keep up with their debt maintenance. However, GSBD's investment at fair value has declined from the prior year's total of $3.47B, which is already an indication of the challenges that the portfolio may be facing. GSBD leans most heavily on first lien debt securities, which is ideal because it is the least risky kind of loan for GSBD.

First lien loans sit at the top of the corporate capital structure and have the highest priority for repayment. So if a borrower is struggling to keep up with their required debt maintenance and needs to liquidate assets, GSBD is first in line to get repaid. This reduces the probability that GSBD will lose all invested capital or face write-downs from a bad investment. The portfolio's breakdown by investment type is as follows:

  • First Lien Debt: 92.8%
  • First Lien, Last-Out Unitranche: 4.1%
  • Second Lien Debt: 1.5%
  • Preferred Stock: 0.8%
  • Common Stock: 0.5%
  • Unsecured Debt: 0.3%
  • Warrants: 0.1%

Expanding on this, GSBD aims to maintain an efficient allocation across different industries. However, GSBD is most heavily allocated to software investments at 17.6% of assets. This may be a problem considering that the market continues to sell off SaaS companies because of the rising threat of AI. The innovations and capabilities within many AI tools have become so efficient that they now threaten the moat for many software companies. However, management addressed these concerns on their latest earnings call and clarified that they remain confident in their ability to select high-quality positions within the software space.

We have been proactively assessing the impacts of AI on the software space for years. We passed on our first deal due to AI concerns in October of 2023 and rolled out an internal framework to evaluate AI disruption risk in early 2025, which is incorporated into all new investments in addition to our ongoing monitoring of existing portfolio exposure. - Vivek Bantwal, Global Head of Financing Group

GSBD reported its Q4 earnings at the end of February, and the results were a bit mixed. Most notably, adjusted net investment income amounted to $0.37 per share, which was a decline from the prior year's net investment income of $0.48 per share. Similarly, total investment income amounted to $86M for the quarter, which was a large reduction from the prior year's total investment income of $103.8M. The main driver to the lower earnings was a reduction in the interest income that GBSD was able to collect from its portfolio of borrowers.

Total interest income amounted to $84.77M for the quarter, which is now the lowest level of interest income collected over the last twelve-month period. A decline in interest income implies that the portfolio is shrinking in size as repayments and sales outpace the rate of new investments. On a more positive note, management was able to slightly offset these declines by reducing its expenses. Total operating expenses amounted to $42.98M, which was a slight reduction from the prior year's operating expenses of $45.75M. However, interest and debt-related expenses increased during the quarter, but GSBD was able to cut its management and incentive fees for the quarter.

However, management can only cut expenses for so long and will eventually need to grow the portfolio through new investments. The increase in interest expenses also implies that GSBD is becoming more reliant on debt to fuel its operations. GSBD holds total debt of $1.87B, which was a slight reduction from the prior year's debt balance of $1.92B. However, the cash position can certainly be improved. For instance, GSBD has cash and equivalents of $43.2M.

GSBD does have $500M of debt that matured in January of this year. With that out of the way, GSBD has ample time to strengthen its balance sheet before the next $400M matures in March of next year. We can see that the majority of GSBD's debt will mature in 2030, which provides plenty of time for substantial improvements to the balance sheet to happen.

Valuation and Risks

At the time of my last coverage, GSBD traded at a discount to NAV of 25.35%. Following the continued pullback in the market, GSBD now trades at a deeper discount to NAV of 28.56%. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that GSBD now trades at the deepest discount to NAV level ever. For reference, GSBD has traded at an average premium to NAV of 1.64% over the last five-year period. While this discount may be enticing for opportunistic investors, I believe that it is actually a reflection of the headwinds that the portfolio is currently facing.

Wall Street analysts currently have an average price target of $9.13 per share, which sits very close to the current market price. While this sort of small upside growth is certainly possible, I have very little confidence that GSBD will deliver any meaningful growth over the next few quarters. Since interest rates remain elevated, management may struggle to find opportunities to grow its base assets and net investment income. This was demonstrated over the last year since GSBD struggled to have new investment activity outpace sales and repayments.

During the most recent quarter, GSBD was able to allocate $394.9M towards new investment commitments. However, this was offset by sales and repayments totaling $251.6M, which resulted in net funded investment activity of $69.5M. While there was still a positive amount of growth during the quarter, it was not enough to drive the needle forward for any meaningful growth. As a result of the weaker earnings and unconvincing investment activity, the underlying NAV has continued to decline for a year straight. For reference, here are the quarter-end NAV amounts:

  • Q4 2024: $13.41 per share
  • Q1 2025: $13.20 per share
  • Q2 2025: $13.02 per share
  • Q3 2025: $12.75 per share
  • Q4 2025: $12.64 per share

Therefore, the risk of NAV erosion remains relevant as long as interest rates stay high. During the Fed's last meeting, it was decided to leave interest rates unchanged. A higher cost of debt will limit the number of companies seeking debt financing in the market and make it harder for GSBD to grow. Similarly, the higher cost of debt will put pressure on existing portfolio companies and can threaten their ability to keep up with debt payments. This can lead to an uptick in the rate of non-accruals.

Non-accruals were reported to be low over the last quarter, now totaling 1.9% of fair portfolio value and 2.8% of cost. This is only a slight uptick from the 1.6% non-accrual rate at the time of my last coverage, so it implies that GSBD's portfolio health has remained solid despite the challenging market conditions. Even when referring to the latest 10-K form, it is confirmed that GSBD's PIK (payment-in-kind) interest income has declined year over year.

PIK interest income totaled $32.9M for 2025, compared to 2024's total PIK interest income of $50.43M. PIK interest income is a measure of income that has been recognized through the additional issuance of debt rather than actual cash flow. It is a measure that allows borrowers to delay cash payments until conditions improve. Therefore, a decline in PIK interest income indicates that the portfolio may have actually improved over the last year.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.32 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 15.8%. As previously mentioned, adjusted net investment income amounted to $0.37 per share for the quarter. This indicates that earnings now support the distribution at a coverage rate of 116%. However, this comes after a 29% reduction in payouts from its prior level of $0.45 per share. Although the new distribution level is supported by earnings at the moment, I would still remain cautious in case the debt markets cause BDCs to deteriorate further.

Any rise in non-accruals or continuous NAV declines may cause management to reduce payouts further. As long as interest rates remain elevated, I believe that GSBD is at risk of seeing its earnings decline. This is especially the case if the rate of new investment activity does not improve over the next few quarters. So while there aren't any glaring red flags that make GSBD a sell today, I would advise caution for income investors seeking a sustainable source of dividend income.

Lastly, investors need to consider the fact that distributions received from GSBD may not offer the most tax-efficient source of income. Traditionally, I would suggest that investors only maintain exposure to BDCs in a tax-advantaged account. However, this may change with new implementations of the One Big Beautiful Bill, which has the potential to provide BDCs with more favorable tax treatment. The bill allows 'qualified' production activities and pass-through income structures to receive a discount on the effective tax rate, similar to REITs. Despite this, I would still hold BDCs in a tax-advantaged account to ensure minimal tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that GSBD is only a hold at this time. The NAV has continued its downward slide as the earnings fail to deliver any meaningful growth and the rate of new investment activity remains lackluster. So despite the large discount to NAV valuation, I do not see any meaningful growth catalysts in the near term. As long as interest rates remain elevated, GSBD may struggle to grow its base assets and earnings. The dividend is covered by earnings at this time, but I would stay cautious just in case earnings decline as a result of rising non-accruals or a reliance on PIK interest income.
